author: Rocky NOW pool
msg: I market them, and then send them to my website, then they sign up for lease, then I email with up to 3 lease codes and directions. I have newsletters I send out with updates. Once we hit opt in jobs with decent pay I have close to 1500 people on my newsletter with 70% open rate who are actually interested. I have had to invest in my marketing both time and money. I pay about 56 cents per lease code filled. Right now I’m loosing money, but my list is most valuable.

author: Crg878
msg:
#AMA Not sure if you’re taking questions for the next AMA, but if not could this be passed on to the devs please.
Just a quick one about the term “proof of work” in the Unity app.
When I first came into this, I kind of assumed it would work a bit like Bitcoin mining. There wasn’t much information around at the time since it was still pretty new and the network hadn’t fully launched, so I didn’t really have much to go on. The phrase “proof of work” basically set that expectation for me.
From my understanding of Bitcoin and similar systems, proof of work means computational effort, and more machines usually means more output because each one is doing independent work.
But Unity seems to work a bit differently. Rewards seem to depend more on things like network uniqueness (IP / carrier / environment), and there does seem to be a point where adding more devices on the same Wi-Fi gives diminishing returns rather than anything scaling in a straightforward way. Even though there’s no stated limit, there definitely feels like a point where extra devices stop adding much value, and it can feel like fewer, more unique connections are actually more effective per device.
So I guess my question is: is “proof of work” really the best term for this, or would something like “proof of network participation” be clearer for people coming from Bitcoin and early crypto where proof of work meant something more computational?
